name: The Seed of the Woman
spokenBy: God, in the judgment on the serpent
summary: The offspring of the woman will crush the serpent's head while being struck himself — read from earliest times as the first gospel promise (the protoevangelium).

fulfillments.note: The New Testament reads Christ's death and resurrection as the serpent-crushing victory

status: fulfilled (as interpreted in the New Testament)

name: Blessing to All Nations through Abraham's Seed
summary: In Abraham and his offspring all the families of the earth will be blessed.

fulfillments.note: Paul identifies the singular 'seed' with Christ; the gospel to the nations as the blessing

status: fulfilled (as interpreted in the New Testament)

name: Four Hundred Years in a Foreign Land
spokenBy: God, in the covenant vision to Abram
summary: Abram's offspring will be sojourners and slaves in a land not theirs four hundred years, then come out with great possessions and return in the fourth generation.

fulfillments.note: The exodus narrative presents the deliverance and the plundering of Egypt as this promise kept

status: fulfilled within the biblical narrative

name: An Everlasting Throne for David's House
summary: God will raise up David's offspring, establish his kingdom, and make his throne endure forever.

fulfillments.note: Immediate horizon: Solomon builds the house
The New Testament proclaims Jesus, son of David, enthroned forever

status: fulfilled in stages (Solomon; ultimately Christ in NT reading)

name: The Immanuel Sign
summary: The virgin (Heb. almah, 'young woman') will conceive and bear a son called Immanuel — 'God with us.'

fulfillments.note: Matthew cites the Greek (Septuagint) parthenos of the virgin birth. Scholarly opinion: many hold a near fulfillment in Isaiah's own day with a fuller sense claimed by Matthew

status: fulfilled (as cited in Matthew; near/far dual reference is discussed among scholars)

name: The Ruler from Bethlehem
summary: From Bethlehem Ephrathah, little among Judah's clans, will come forth a ruler whose origins are from of old.

fulfillments.note: The chief priests cite this to Herod when the magi ask where the Christ is born

status: fulfilled (as cited in the New Testament)

name: A Great Light in Galilee
summary: The land of Zebulun and Naphtali, Galilee of the nations, once in gloom, will see a great light.

fulfillments.note: Matthew applies the oracle to Jesus settling in Capernaum and beginning the Galilean ministry

status: fulfilled (as cited in Matthew)

name: The Voice in the Wilderness
summary: A voice cries: prepare the way of the LORD; the messenger comes before him, in the spirit of Elijah.

fulfillments.note: All four Gospels identify John the Baptist as the voice; Jesus names him the Elijah to come

status: fulfilled (as cited in the Gospels)

name: The King Comes on a Donkey
summary: Zion's king comes righteous and victorious, humble and mounted on a donkey, on a colt.

fulfillments.note: Cited at the triumphal entry on Palm Sunday

status: fulfilled (as cited in the Gospels)

name: Thirty Pieces of Silver
summary: The shepherd's wage — thirty pieces of silver — is thrown to the potter in the house of the LORD.

fulfillments.note: Judas' betrayal price and the potter's field; Matthew's citation blends Zechariah with Jeremiah's imagery

status: fulfilled (as cited in Matthew)

name: The Suffering Servant
summary: The servant is despised, pierced for our transgressions, silent before his shearers, numbered with transgressors, buried with the rich, and yet will see his offspring and justify many.

fulfillments.note: The passage Philip expounds to the Ethiopian official 'beginning with this Scripture... the good news about Jesus.' Jewish interpretation has historically read the servant corporately of Israel — a live scholarly discussion

status: fulfilled (in New Testament reading; interpretation of the servant is discussed)

name: The Psalm of the Forsaken One
summary: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' — mocked by onlookers, hands and feet pierced, garments divided by lot; yet ending in praise before the nations.

fulfillments.note: Jesus prays the psalm's opening from the cross; the Gospels note the divided garments

status: fulfilled (as cited in the Gospels)

name: Not Abandoned to the Grave
summary: 'You will not abandon my soul to Sheol, or let your holy one see corruption.'

fulfillments.note: Peter at Pentecost and Paul at Pisidian Antioch argue David died and was buried — the psalm speaks of the Christ's resurrection

status: fulfilled (as cited in Acts)

name: The New Covenant
summary: Days are coming when God will make a new covenant — law written on hearts, sins remembered no more.

fulfillments.note: 'This cup is the new covenant in my blood' — the words of institution take up Jeremiah's promise

status: fulfilled (as claimed at the Last Supper and in Hebrews)

name: The Spirit Poured on All Flesh
summary: Afterward God will pour out his Spirit on all flesh — sons and daughters prophesying — and everyone who calls on the name of the LORD will be saved.

fulfillments.note: 'This is what was uttered through the prophet Joel' — Peter's Pentecost sermon

status: fulfilled at Pentecost (as cited by Peter)

name: Seventy Years for Babylon
summary: Judah will serve Babylon seventy years; then Babylon will be punished and the exiles brought home.

fulfillments.note: Cyrus' decree 'that the word of the LORD by Jeremiah might be fulfilled'; Daniel counts the years in prayer. Approx. 605–538 BC (first deportation to decree) or 586–516 BC (temple to temple)

status: fulfilled within the biblical narrative (538 BC)

name: Cyrus Named as Restorer
summary: God names Cyrus as his 'shepherd' and 'anointed' who will subdue nations and order Jerusalem and the temple rebuilt.

fulfillments.note: Cyrus' decree of 538 BC. Scholarly opinion: critical scholarship dates Isaiah 40–55 to the exile (naming a contemporary), while conservative scholarship holds eighth-century predictive prophecy — presented here as recorded

status: fulfilled within the biblical narrative (dating of the oracle is debated)

name: Josiah Named at Bethel's Altar
spokenBy: An unnamed man of God from Judah
summary: Against Jeroboam's new altar at Bethel: a son named Josiah, born to the house of David, will defile this altar — some three centuries in advance.

fulfillments.note: Josiah defiles the Bethel altar in his reform, 'according to the word of the LORD that the man of God proclaimed'

status: fulfilled within the biblical narrative (approx. 622 BC)

name: The Fall of the Northern Kingdom
summary: Israel will go into exile beyond Damascus; Samaria's calf will be carried to Assyria and her king cut off.

fulfillments.note: Samaria fell to Assyria in 722 BC; the narrative explicitly recalls the prophets' warnings

status: fulfilled within the biblical narrative (722 BC; historically corroborated by Assyrian records)

name: Sennacherib Will Not Enter the City
summary: The king of Assyria will not enter Jerusalem or shoot an arrow there; he will return the way he came.

fulfillments.note: The army is struck in the night and Sennacherib withdraws, later assassinated at Nineveh. Archaeological evidence: Sennacherib's prism claims tribute but records no capture of Jerusalem

status: fulfilled within the biblical narrative (701 BC)

name: Jerusalem Given to Babylon
summary: Because Judah would not listen, Nebuchadnezzar will take the city; it will be burned and the land left desolate.

fulfillments.note: Jerusalem fell in 586 BC. Archaeological evidence: Babylonian Chronicle for 597 BC; burn layers and Lachish letters for the final campaign

status: fulfilled within the biblical narrative (586 BC; historically corroborated)

name: The Oracle against Tyre
summary: Many nations will come against Tyre; her stones and timber will be cast into the sea and her site scraped bare like a rock.

fulfillments.note: Historical fulfillment: Nebuchadnezzar besieged mainland Tyre thirteen years (Ezekiel itself notes the limited spoil); Alexander the Great in 332 BC built a causeway from the mainland rubble to take the island city — recorded by ancient historians (Arrian, Curtius)

status: fulfilled historically (staged: Babylon, then Greece)

name: The Four Kingdoms
spokenBy: Daniel, interpreting Nebuchadnezzar's dream and his own visions
summary: A succession of four kingdoms — gold, silver, bronze, iron — after which the God of heaven sets up a kingdom that will never be destroyed.

fulfillments.note: Common interpretation: Babylon → Medo-Persia → Greece → Rome, with God's kingdom in Christ. Scholarly opinion: critical scholarship reads the visions from a second-century BC Maccabean vantage (Babylon–Media–Persia–Greece); conservative scholarship holds sixth-century predictive prophecy

status: interpreted of the empire succession (interpretation and dating debated)

name: The Seventy Weeks
summary: Seventy 'weeks' are decreed: from the word to restore Jerusalem until an anointed one, atonement made, and desolations decreed.

fulfillments.note: Scholarly opinion: interpretations divide sharply — messianic readings count to Christ's ministry and death; critical readings center on the Antiochene crisis (167–164 BC); others see patterns extending further. Listed as a prophecy whose interpretation is debated rather than assigned a single fulfillment

status: interpretation debated

name: Not One Stone upon Another
spokenBy: Jesus, on the Mount of Olives
summary: Of the temple buildings: not one stone will be left upon another; Jerusalem will be surrounded by armies and trodden down.

fulfillments.note: Historical fulfillment: Titus' legions destroyed the temple in AD 70, recorded in detail by Josephus (Jewish War 6)

status: fulfilled historically (AD 70)

name: Before the Rooster Crows
summary: Before the rooster crows, Peter will deny Jesus three times.

fulfillments.note: Peter denies three times in the high priest's courtyard and weeps bitterly

status: fulfilled within the biblical narrative

name: The Son of Man Raised on the Third Day
summary: The Son of Man must suffer, be killed, and on the third day be raised; 'destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up.'

fulfillments.note: The angel at the tomb: 'he has risen, as he said' — the disciples remember his words

status: fulfilled within the biblical narrative

name: The Flood Announced
summary: God announces the end of all flesh and instructs Noah to build the ark — 'a herald of righteousness' to his generation.

fulfillments.note: The flood comes as announced; Noah and his household preserved

status: fulfilled within the biblical narrative

name: They Will Look on Him Whom They Pierced
summary: They will look on me, on him whom they have pierced, and mourn as for an only child.

fulfillments.note: John cites it of the spear-thrust at the cross; Revelation extends it to the coming of Christ

status: fulfilled (as cited in John; Revelation applies it forward)

name: Out of Egypt I Called My Son
summary: 'When Israel was a child, I loved him, and out of Egypt I called my son' — spoken of the exodus.

fulfillments.note: Matthew reads Jesus' return from Egypt as recapitulating Israel's story — a typological (pattern) fulfillment rather than simple prediction

status: fulfilled typologically (as cited in Matthew)

name: Not One of His Bones Broken
spokenBy: David, in psalm; and the Passover statute
summary: The righteous one's bones are kept — not one broken; the Passover lamb's bones must not be broken.

fulfillments.note: The soldiers break the others' legs but find Jesus already dead — John cites both the psalm and the Passover rule, presenting Jesus as the true lamb

status: fulfilled (as cited in John)

name: A Priest Forever after Melchizedek
summary: 'The LORD says to my Lord: sit at my right hand'; 'you are a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek.'

fulfillments.note: The most-quoted psalm in the New Testament: Jesus poses it to the Pharisees; Hebrews builds its priesthood argument on Melchizedek

status: fulfilled (as interpreted in the New Testament)
